nativeRange.habitat: Upper edges of steep outer reef slopes with moderate currents; forms large mixed-sex aggregations in open water above coral.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical reef-associated planktivore zone above outer-reef drop-offs.

decorPreferences: open swimming space above live rock

diet.notes: In the wild, the Dispar Anthias feeds on zooplankton drifting through the open water column. In captivity it is generally advised to receive 3–5 small feedings per day of mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and high-quality pellet or flake foods. A refugium that cultivates and exports copepods and amphipods can help supplement steady grazing between feedings. Newly introduced specimens may initially refuse prepared foods and tend to do best when offered a variety of frozen items first.

imageCandidates.notes: Nick Hobgood (Nhobgood), 1500x2000. In-situ reef shoal of vivid orange-red Pseudanthias dispar showing the harem/shoaling behaviour and red-edged dorsals; best represents how this haremic planktivore is kept and displayed. Recommended.

Rickard Zerpe, 3669x2752. Tack-sharp lateral view of a male with the crimson anterior dorsal fin (the 'redfin' diagnostic) fully raised, yellow flank and electric-blue fin margins; whole fish in frame.
Rickard Zerpe, 2395x1796. Crisp side profile of a vibrant male showing pink head, yellow-orange back, red dorsal flag and blue-edged lyretail; a second distinct individual for gallery variety.

summary: The Dispar Anthias is a peaceful, planktivorous reef fish whose males display vivid red dorsal fins and a bright orange-red body, while females are a softer yellowish-orange. Best suited to medium-to-large reef tanks, it requires multiple small feedings each day and a tightly fitted lid to prevent jumping.
careNotes: Dispar Anthias are open-water planktivores that have been documented thriving with ample horizontal swimming space above the rockwork; a tank length of about 120 cm or more is generally advised. They are constant grazers and tend to decline rapidly without 3–5 small feedings per day, ideally of varied frozen mysis, brine, and finely chopped seafoods. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: all individuals begin as females, and the dominant female of a harem will transition into a male when no male is present. Best kept singly, as a single male with a harem of 4–10 females, or as a small group of all females in suitably sized tanks; multiple males are commonly considered incompatible due to territorial conflict. Reports of jumping are common, so a tightly-sealed lid is strongly advised. Generally considered reef-safe with corals and ornamental invertebrates. Newly imported specimens have been known to refuse prepared foods initially and may need live or heavily enriched frozen offerings before accepting a wider diet. The species has been reclassified into the genus Nemanthias and is now formally known as Nemanthias dispar, though it continues to be sold under the older name Pseudanthias dispar throughout the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has rarely been reported. The Dispar Anthias is a pelagic broadcast spawner, releasing eggs into open water where they drift as plankton — a reproductive strategy that makes larvae very difficult to rear in aquarium conditions. Sex change from female to male occurs naturally in a group whenever the dominant male is removed from the harem.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source for sizing, water parameters, temperament, and reef-safety. States 70 gal minimum (single) / 125 gal for harem, 4 in max size, 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, peaceful, reef-safe, carnivore.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (now Nemanthias dispar, family Anthiadidae), max length 9.5 cm TL, depth 0-18 m typically 1-15 m, distribution from Christmas Island to Line Islands south to Great Barrier Reef, Fiji and Samoa. Trophic level 3.3.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (300 L minimum, ~26 C, SG ~1.026), reef-safe status, jumping risk (known to jump out of open aquaria), and harem behavior (8+ recommended to reduce subordinate stress). Confirmed multiple-daily-feeding requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner that releases eggs into open water. Larvae are rarely if ever successfully reared in captivity.
